Anthony LaMonica, Diamond Member. And let me see if I can set this up correctly. I want to put this fascinating day yesterday so far. I think a lot of people agree. You've said a big statement from me. You said a couple of years ago, I first heard it, that there's basically only two kinds of buyers that a buyer, that a business can get. People who are ready to buy now, and then people who are not ready to buy now. They may be. And you said that you can't build a business or make a fortune only off of the people who are ready to buy now in any market because there's never enough of them. So the game becomes convincing and putting yourself in front of the people who might be interested in selling to them and getting enough of them. You said of buyers and prospects generally, that it's your system of selling versus their system for buying. It's basically an adversarial relationship. And that most people's system for buying, their default system, is to not buy. That was right. Is to not buy. So these three days, basically the way to trump that system to not buy. Yeah, there are big steps in that direction. So we take what you just said, which is all an accurate reflection. In any business, there are ready and even determined to buy now buyers. So they have no system whatsoever to capture the secondary buyer, the almost persuaded buyer. So think about like the bread making machine and a William Sonoma store. For everybody who walks in there and buys one, either predetermined, I'm looking for a bread making machine, or accidentally, oh, chef, that'd be nice to make bread on Sunday. How many people stand there for five minutes, look at the machine, think about it, there's a conversation, and they leave without buying a bread making machine. Right? Now, today's tech world, for example, at the retail level, we could have a scan do-hicking next to the machine, they scan this, and we'll send you a seven minute video about five different ways to use the bread making machine, and boom, we got a list. Right? So it's doable in almost every business at almost every level, but for the most part, nobody does. There's also a secular religion in many businesses that gets in the way. So like all car guys believe, and when I work with Miracle Air, all Miracle Air guys, because they're basically car guys, all car guys believe there are no B-backs. That's how it's said in the car business. If you get an old kind of car guy, he's going to say there ain't no B-backs, meaning you either close them while they're here or you ain't going to get them. All the empirical data about the car business contradicts this. There's abundant evidence, factual proof, that the overwhelming majority of car buyers buy on the third weekend, and they buy by going back to three or more places they were at once, then narrowing those three down to one and buying. The whole industry has it happening by happenstance. Right? Nobody has it happening. And it converted this morning 15% more than what usually does. And I spent the same amount of money, same traffic source. And they're buying. And these are people who have been following for a while. They just became buyers last night. So you can take the mic back. Okay. So you want to do two things, right? You want to improve the buy now. And you want to consistently be asking yourself, how can we improve the buy now? Right? And what techniques can we apply to the people who aren't buying now? Right? To move them. And the use of the word efficient is interesting. I don't know how deliberate you were. But it's way up there. Because so, like in selling to parents, for example, proactive, orthodontics, Invisalign, and so on, virtually all parents are now walking around with a guilt complex. And it has manipulated all the time against them about their kids. So and if they're strife in the family, then one of them is guiltier than the other. If it's a divorce, if there's been a divorce in the family, there's an enormous amount of guilt on one side or the other. And so it's a very pokeable button. And most people, if not guilt, they feel some inappropriateness, some discomfort about thoughts or feelings or behaviors that they engage in that can be probed. So if we're selling B2B, if we're selling what we just talked about, so let's say we're selling some version of a no fail follow up system, partee, whole conversion management platform, infusion soft used for that purpose, you name it. Look, so everybody, and I don't want to pick on you, but so this is like the question that is asked all the time that should never be asked, because it is the relentless search for an incredibly simple answer to an incredibly complex opportunity or problem. It is our desire for a blue pill. That's it. And that's not how fortunes are made. It's not how great success is created. So Kapoozie asked me about the list. Now the list you've got is not until now a list I use. It is a composite made for this seminar from other lists I use. And specific to copywriting, Tamal's topic, I no longer work from the list in front. I use the list at the end to check myself just to see if I've missed something because it is so ingrained. But so I actually am working from more than what I just gave you, not less. And yes, situationally, there's a big opportunity to emphasize this versus this, this grouping of ideas versus this grouping of ideas. But to generalize that and say, OK, there's 50. And let's put them in clumps of the 10 most powerful and the 10 second most powerful and the 10 third most powerful. If that's doable, I don't know how to do it. So it is more about how can we exert maximum leverage on this person in order to push them in the direction, pull them in the direction we want them to go. Which by the way, gets all the way to length of presentation, which is one of the last things that we're going to talk about today. He's drawn the pyramids. He's done the math. He gets it all done, right? Well, what do you all think? And kind of the leader of the group, the old Amish guy says, not sure, son, slow that down by half and run it by us one more time. And so there's pace, right? Different people individually or in groups, they communicate at a certain pace. If you're with a handsy person, it's useful, you're someone doing Italian. It's useful to be gesturing. If you're with a Brit, too much of that.
